data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Steel Dynamics (STLD) After A 68% One Year Rally?
data.description:
• If you are asking whether Steel Dynamics at US$194.11 still offers value or is pricing in too much optimism, the starting point is to look closely at how its current share price lines up with different valuation checks.
  • The stock has returned 3.5% over the past 7 days, 11.4% over 30 days, 10.3% year to date, and 67.8% over 1 year, which naturally raises questions about what is already reflected in the price.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Steel Dynamics as a key name in US-listed steel producers, with attention on how its position in the sector relates to industrial activity and capital spending. Commentary around the stock often highlights how investors are weighing sector specific factors against broader materials sentiment when interpreting these returns.

Approach 1: Steel Dynamics Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes expected future cash flows, then discounts them back to today to estimate what the business might be worth right now. It is essentially a way of translating future cash generation into a single present value figure.

For Steel Dynamics, Simply Wall St applies a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model using cash flow projections expressed in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow is reported as a loss of about $42.3 million, so the model leans heavily on expected future free cash flow rather than recent figures. Analyst and extrapolated estimates suggest free cash flow reaching about $3.3 billion in 2035, with intermediate projections such as $2.0 billion in 2026 and $2.6 billion in 2028, all discounted back to today using the DCF framework.

On this basis, the estimated intrinsic value comes out at roughly $310.17 per share, compared with the current share price of about $194.11. That implies a 37.4% discount, which indicates that Steel Dynamics is trading below this model-based estimate of fair value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Steel Dynamics is undervalued by 37.4%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 60 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: Steel Dynamics Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful guide because it links what you pay for the stock to the earnings the business is currently generating. The higher the expected earnings growth and the lower the perceived risk, the more investors are often willing to pay in terms of a higher P/E multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually supports a lower, more conservative P/E.

Steel Dynamics currently trades on a P/E of 23.7x. This sits above the peer group average of 20.2x and also above the broader Metals and Mining industry average of 22.9x. This suggests the market is applying a somewhat richer earnings multiple than these simple benchmarks. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Steel Dynamics is 27.4x, which is the P/E level implied by its model after factoring in elements such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market size and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a straight peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for those underlying differences rather than assuming all companies deserve the same multiple. Since the Fair Ratio of 27.4x is above the current P/E of 23.7x, this approach suggests the shares may be trading below that model based earnings multiple.

Result: UNDERVALUED

For example, one investor might build a narrative around strong infrastructure demand, 6.8% forecast revenue growth, an 8.3% profit margin and a Fair Value close to the current analyst consensus of US$188.25. data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Revolution Medicines (RVMD) After A 321.4% One Year Surge?
data.description:
• Wondering if Revolution Medicines at around US$152.54 is priced for perfection or still leaves room for value? This article looks at what the current market price may be implying.
  • The stock has seen sharp moves recently, with returns of 57.7% over 7 days, 54.1% over 30 days, 93.0% year to date and 321.4% over the past year. These moves can reshape how investors think about both upside and risk.

That backdrop helps explain why such strong recent returns are drawing in new interest as well as closer scrutiny of the price.
  • Even with this excitement, the company currently holds a valuation score of 2 out of 6. Next up is a look at standard valuation approaches and how they compare with a more complete way to think about value later in the article.

Revolution Medicines scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Revolution Medicines Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to a present value using a required rate of return.

For Revolution Medicines, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow stands at a loss of about US$913.7 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections in the model show free cash flow moving from losses in the coming years to a projected US$1,248.1 million in 2030, with further estimates extending out to 2035 based on those earlier inputs.

Putting all of those projected cash flows together, Simply Wall St’s DCF output arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about US$509.01 per share. Compared with the current share price of around US$152.54, this implies the shares are about 70.0% below that modelled value. Within this framework, the stock is treated as undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Revolution Medicines is undervalued by 70.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 60 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Revolution Medicines.

Approach 2: Revolution Medicines Price vs Book

For companies that are still loss making or reinvesting heavily, earnings based metrics such as the P/E ratio can be less helpful. In those cases, balance sheet based measures like the price to book, or P/B, ratio are often used instead, because they compare the share price with the accounting value of the company’s net assets.

Growth expectations and risk still matter for P/B. A higher growth outlook or stronger perceived competitive position can justify investors paying a higher multiple of book value, while higher risk, uncertainty or weaker profitability usually point to a lower, more conservative multiple as a rough guide to what might be considered “normal” for a stock.

Revolution Medicines currently trades at a P/B of 18.53x. That sits well above the Biotechs industry average of about 2.37x and also above the peer group average of 9.53x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what a suitable P/B might be once factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks are considered together. Because it aims to tailor the multiple to the company’s own profile instead of relying only on broad peer or industry comparisons, it can give a more tailored view of whether the current P/B looks stretched or conservative. In this case, with no separate Fair Ratio provided to compare against the actual 18.53x, the multiple simply looks high relative to both sector and peers without a clear model based anchor.

Result: OVERVALUED

This lets you see, for example, how one investor might build a bullish Narrative around a Fair Value near US$170.0, while another takes a more cautious view closer to US$73.0, and decide for yourself which version of the story feels closer to what you think is realistic.

  • Highlights meaningful risks around trial outcomes, concentration in RAS cancers, execution across many global studies and commercial build out that could challenge those optimistic assumptions.

🐻 Revolution Medicines Bear Case
